items.title: Frequently Asked Questions
items.text:
What is docwalk?

Your codebase, documented. Automatically. Analyze any repo, generate MkDocs Material sites, deploy anywhere, and keep docs in sync with git-diff intelligence.

What languages/technologies does docwalk use?

typescript (100%).

What commands are available?

Available commands: docwalk ci-setup, docwalk deploy, docwalk dev, docwalk doctor, docwalk generate, docwalk init, docwalk status, docwalk sync, docwalk undeploy, docwalk version-list, docwalk version-deploy. See the Getting Started guide for usage details.

Where can I find the developer documentation?

See the Developer Reference section for architecture, API reference, and source documentation.

Generated by DocWalk

items.title: Features
items.text: A comprehensive guide to everything docwalk can do.

items.title: docwalk ci-setup
items.text: Generates provider-specific CI/CD pipeline configuration. Supports both production deploy and PR preview workflows.

items.title: docwalk deploy
items.text: Orchestrates: build → provider auth check → deploy → domain setup. Provides user-friendly errors when external tools are missing.

items.title: docwalk dev
items.text: Starts Zensical dev server for local preview. If Zensical isn't installed, auto-installs it into .docwalk/venv/ (no system pip needed).

items.title: docwalk doctor
items.text: Checks prerequisites and installs Zensical into a managed venv at .docwalk/venv/ — no system-wide pip install needed.

items.title: docwalk generate
items.text: Runs the full analysis → generation pipeline: 1. Load config (auto-init if missing) 2. Analyze codebase (with ora spinners) 3. Generate MkDocs Material site

items.title: docwalk init
items.text: Interactive setup wizard with two tracks: Quick Start — 3 steps: confirm repo, AI setup, theme → generate Custom — Full 6-step wizard (repo, analysis, deploy, domain, sync, theme)

items.title: docwalk status
items.text: Shows current sync state, project info, and config summary.

items.title: docwalk sync
items.text: Runs incremental sync: git-diff → targeted re-analysis → rebuild.

items.title: docwalk undeploy
items.text: Removes a deployed documentation site from the hosting provider.

items.title: docwalk version-list
items.text: List available versions from git tags matching the configured pattern.

items.title: docwalk version-deploy
items.text:
Deploy a specific version tag using mike.

Options:

  • --tag

AI Analysis

To address the issue of the orphan module, I recommend adding an import statement to the module to explicitly declare its dependency.

Since the file is a type declaration file (.d.ts), no changes are needed to the existing code. However, to resolve the issue, you can add a comment to indicate that the module is intentionally left as a global declaration:

// Intentionally left as a global declaration
declare module "@docwalk/themes-premium" {
  export function register(licenseKey?: string): void;
}

Alternatively, consider removing the file if it's not being used elsewhere in the codebase.

No code changes are required in this case, but a review of the codebase is necessary to ensure the module is not being used.

If used, then no action required.

However an eslint rule can be added for no-unused-vars to catch similar issues.

items.title: Getting Started with Docwalk
items.text: Welcome to Docwalk! We're excited to help you get started with this powerful tool. In this tutorial, we'll walk you through the installation process and a practical first use case.

items.title: Installation
items.text:
To install Docwalk, follow these steps:

  1. Make sure you have the necessary prerequisites: Docwalk requires a compatible operating system and a package manager. Since your package manager is listed as "unknown", you might need to install a package manager first or use a different installation method. For most users, installing via a package manager like Homebrew (on macOS) or apt (on Linux) is recommended. However, specific installation commands may vary.
  2. Install Docwalk: Run the following command in your terminal:
# This command might vary based on your package manager
# For example, if you're on macOS with Homebrew, it could look like this:
brew install docwalk

# Or, if you're on Linux with apt:
sudo apt-get install docwalk

# If you're using a different package manager or method, replace this line accordingly

If you're unsure about the installation command for your system, please consult the official Docwalk documentation or community forums for more detailed instructions.

items.title: First Use Walkthrough
items.text: Now that Docwalk is installed, let's go through a practical first use case.

items.title: Step 1: Initialize a New Docwalk Project
items.text:
To start using Docwalk, you need to initialize a new project. Run the following command in your terminal:

docwalk init my-docwalk-project

Replace my-docwalk-project with the name of your project.

items.title: Step 2: Generate Documentation
items.text:
Next, let's generate some documentation for your project. Navigate into your project directory:

cd my-docwalk-project

Now, run the following command to generate some sample documentation:

docwalk generate

This command will create a basic documentation structure for your project.

items.title: Step 3: Develop Your Documentation
items.text:
To start developing your documentation, run the following command:

docwalk dev

This command will start a local development server, allowing you to preview your documentation in real-time.

items.title: Step 4: Deploy Your Documentation
items.text:
Once you're happy with your documentation, you can deploy it. First, you need to set up your deployment environment using:

docwalk ci-setup

Follow the prompts to configure your deployment environment. After setting up your CI environment, you can deploy your documentation:

docwalk deploy

Congratulations! You've successfully deployed your documentation using Docwalk.

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Run docwalk status to check the status of your project.
  • Use docwalk sync to synchronize your documentation with your project.
  • Explore more commands and features in the Docwalk documentation.

We're excited to see what you'll create with Docwalk! If you have any questions or need help, don't hesitate to reach out to our community forums.

items.title: Welcome to Docwalk!
items.text: Docwalk is a powerful tool that helps you manage and deploy your documentation projects with ease. It's designed to make your life simpler, whether you're a writer, developer, or project manager.

items.title: Who is Docwalk for?
items.text:
Docwalk is perfect for anyone who creates and maintains documentation projects, such as:

  • Technical writers who need to publish and update documentation
  • Developers who want to automate documentation deployment
  • Project managers who oversee documentation projects

Docwalk solves common problems like:

  • Manually uploading and updating documentation files
  • Keeping track of different versions of documentation
  • Ensuring documentation is deployed correctly across multiple environments

items.title: Main Capabilities
items.text:
Here are some of the key features you can expect from Docwalk:

  • Easy setup and configuration: Get started with Docwalk in minutes using simple CLI commands like ci-setup and init.
  • Version control and deployment: Manage different versions of your documentation and deploy them to various environments using commands like version-deploy and deploy.
  • Automated syncing and status updates: Keep your documentation in sync across environments and get instant status updates with commands like sync and status.
  • Troubleshooting and diagnostics: Use the doctor command to quickly identify and fix common issues.

items.title: Getting Started

async function summarizeModules(
  options: SummarizeOptions
): Promise<SummarizeResult>

Run AI summarization on all modules.

Run AI summarization on all modules. Uses batched calls: one API request per module summarizes both the module and all its exported symbols. This reduces API call count from O(modules * symbols) to O(modules), which is more efficient regardless of backend. Fault-tolerant: - If the API key is missing, returns modules unchanged - If individual API calls fail, those summaries are skipped - Cached summaries are reused when content hasn't changed - Retry with backoff handles transient network errors

Name Type Default Description options SummarizeOptions

items.title: structure-advisor.ts
items.text:
Structure Advisor

Structure Advisor AI-driven page structure suggestions. Analyzes the repo manifest and suggests which pages to generate and how to organize navigation. Falls back to the fixed page list when AI is unavailable. When an AIProvider is available, sends the file tree, README, and module summaries to the LLM and parses an XML wiki structure back — similar to DeepWiki's approach where the model decides what pages to create, which files belong to each page, importance levels, and cross-page relationships.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - `src/analysis/structure-advisor.ts` — this module (338 lines) Source src/analysis/structure-advisor.ts Language TypeScript Lines 338 Exports 4

AI Summary

Provides AI-driven page structure suggestions by analyzing the repo manifest and suggesting which pages to generate and how to organize navigation, exposing PageSuggestion, SectionSuggestion, and StructurePlan interfaces that represent the suggested structure, consumed by the generators to create documentation.

When no provider is available (or on LLM/parse failure), falls back to an empty plan so the default page set is used.

items.text:
Text Parser — Fallback

AI Summary

Orchestrates the fallback parser pipeline by exposing createParser() and parse() for lightweight file analysis. Consumed by the CLI layer to report file existence and extract basic descriptions from comment blocks. Does not attempt AST symbol extraction. This module provides a minimal parser implementation for file types without a full tree-sitter parser.

items.text:
BM25 Text Search Engine

BM25 Text Search Engine Lightweight full-text search that runs in both Node.js and the browser. Used by the Q&A widget to find relevant documentation chunks without needing an embedding API call at query time. BM25 (Best Matching 25) scores documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - `src/qa/text-search.ts` — this module (209 lines) Source src/qa/text-search.ts Language TypeScript Lines 209 Exports 7

AI Summary

Implements a lightweight BM25 text search engine for full-text search in both Node.js and browser environments. Exposes the buildTextIndex() and searchText() functions, along with interfaces for TextSearchIndex and TextSearchResult. This module enables efficient text search capabilities for the Q&A widget without requiring embedding API calls at query time, complementing the vector search functionality. It operates on chunked content prepared by the chunker module.

items.title: engine.ts
items.text:
DocWalk Sync Engine

DocWalk Sync Engine Implements the git-diff based incremental sync strategy. Compares current HEAD against the last indexed commit SHA, identifies changed files, runs targeted re-analysis, and triggers incremental doc rebuild + deploy. Hardened to handle: corrupt state files, force pushes where the previous commit no longer exists, file renames, recursive impact analysis, and manual commit range diffing via --since.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - `src/sync/engine.ts` — this module (478 lines) Source src/sync/engine.ts Language TypeScript Lines 478 Exports 2

AI Summary

Orchestrates the incremental sync pipeline by implementing a git-diff based strategy. Exposes runSync() and SyncOptions for configuration and execution. Consumed by the CLI layer to enable targeted re-analysis and incremental doc rebuild + deploy. Handles various edge cases such as corrupt state files and force pushes.
